coredata.dc:title: Preparation of fluorescent carbon dots and their interaction with human-transferrin (hTf)
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zedi
Wang, Xiaomiao
Zhang, Xiufeng
Yu, Peng
Geng, Lina
Shi, Shikao
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) are new fluorescent materials and widely used in biomedical fields. In this paper, CDs were prepared using citric acid and glycine (denoted G-CDs) by one-step microwave-assisted method and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), power X-ray diffraction (XRD), nuclear magnetic resonance (13CNMR), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). The results showed that glycine was better than PEG200 or hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) as the modification in reaction. The prepared G-CDs had the sizes about 10nm, high fluorescent quantum yield (QY) up to 31%, and stability at biological system. Moreover, G-CDs had low cytotoxicity. Our results verified that amino group (or secondary amines) had a significant effect on the QY; one-step microwave-assisted method was simple, efficient and green. The further experiments about the interaction of G-CDs with human transferrin (hTf) were also detected. Data showed that there was a strong interaction between G-CDs and hTf, and one type of binding site between them. The quenching mode of fluorescence of hTf with G-CDs was static quenching process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Amorphous materials
A. Carbides
B. Optical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selection modification reagent and cell cytotoxicity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31201305 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Colour coordinate (a), Cytotoxicity (b) and TEM micrographs (c: G-CDs, d: P-CDs, e: C-CDs) of CDs.
Characterizations of G-CDs. UV absorption and fluorescent emission spectra (a), Emission spectra at different excitations (b), FT-IR spectrum (c), 13CNMR spectrum (d) and XRD pattern (e).

Emitting light of G-CDs under fluorescence microscopy. Lens paper was dropped with water (control): light field (a), fluorescence field (b, excited at 350nm); lens paper was dropped with G-CDs: light field (c), fluorescence field (d, excited at 350nm).

The interaction of G-CDs with hTf (chTf =5μM; pH=7.4): UV–vis spectra (a), Fluorescence spectra (λex =280nm) (b), Stern-Volmer plots of fluorescence quenching (c), Plots of lg[(F0 −F)/F] versus lg[Q] (d).
Spectral features and QYs of CDs

head.ce:title: Preparation of fluorescent carbon dots and their interaction with human-transferrin (hTf)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hebei United University, Tangshan 063009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glycine–modified carbon dots(G-CDs) were prepared using citric acid and glycine by one-step microwave-assisted method. G-CDs were about 10nm, amorphous, showed blue fluorescence with high QY (31%), and low cytotoxicity to caco-2 cells. Two kinds of hybridized carbon, sp2 and sp3 appeared in G-CDs. There was a strong interaction between G-CDs and hTf, one type of binding site between them, and the quenching mode was static quenching process.

Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) are new fluorescent materials and widely used in biomedical fields. In this paper, CDs were prepared using citric acid and glycine (denoted G-CDs) by one-step microwave-assisted method and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), power X-ray diffraction (XRD), nuclear magnetic resonance (13CNMR), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). The results showed that glycine was better than PEG200 or hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) as the modification in reaction. The prepared G-CDs had the sizes about 10nm, high fluorescent quantum yield (QY) up to 31%, and stability at biological system. Moreover, G-CDs had low cytotoxicity. Our results verified that amino group (or secondary amines) had a significant effect on the QY; one-step microwave-assisted method was simple, efficient and green. The further experiments about the interaction of G-CDs with human transferrin (hTf) were also detected. Data showed that there was a strong interaction between G-CDs and hTf, and one type of binding site between them. The quenching mode of fluorescence of hTf with G-CDs was static quenching process.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of heavy-metal-free fluorescent nanomaterials as novel probes has drawn increasing attention in recent years [1]. Carbon-based nanomaterial is one of the most important materials. Among them carbon dots (CDs) have exceptional advantages, such as high optical absorption, strong and tunable photoluminescence, chemical stability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [2], which make them as the promising candidates for bioimaging [3], medical diagnosis [4], analyte detection [5], catalysis [6], and photovoltaic devices [7], temperature probes [8] and humidity probes [9].

Significant efforts have been devoted to the synthesis of the CDs since CDs discovered from carbon nanotubes during the process of electrophoresis in 2004 [10]. In recent years, a variety of synthesis methods including arc discharge [11], laser ablation [12], electrochemical oxidation [13], acid dehydration [14], ultrasonic treatment [15], microwave-assisted [16] and hydrothermal [17] method have been developed. Microwave-assisted method is regarded as an economical and green method due to its low-cost, efficient, facile, reduced fabrication time and improved product yields. In addition, microwave irradiation is unique in speeding up the reaction kinetics and easy to scale-up without suffering thermal gradient effects [18]. However, microwave-assisted method has a significant drawback that is the low quantum yield (QY) [16,18].

It was reported that carbon source and modification reagents are important characteristics, which can determine the properties of CDs, such as structure, property and QY. Now, many carbon source have been tried, including graphite [19], carbon nanotubes [20], carbohydrates (glucose, sucrose, lactose, etc) [21,22], gelatin [23], bovine serum [24], coffee grounds [25], bamboo leaf [26] and so on. Odriozola Yan et al. pointed out carboxyl-rich group could make CDs exhibit good dispersion and photo-stability in aqueous solution [27]. Wang et al. reported that passivation agents can influence the surface composition, charge, stability and activity of the CDs [28]. Wu et al. used pipe tobacco to prepare nitrogen-containing CDs which can be directly used for detection of Cu2+ without any modification [29]. Sun and co-authors observed that the emission peaks of N, S co-doped CDs shifted to longer wavelengths with the different amount of modification reagents [30]. Hu et al. reported that modification of nitrogen and chlorine on CDs’ surface can influence the photoluminescence and photocatalytic activity [31].

The unique properties of CDs determine their potential application in vivo, and the usage in bioimaging [3], medical diagnosis [4] and drug-delivery [32] have been widely reported. Transferrin (Tf) is one of the most transport proteins in the serum and strongly binds to many small molecules. But little reports about whether or not CDs can bind transferrin, so the interaction model and mechanism of CDs to Tf is important to the application of CDs in vivo in the future.

In this paper, citric acid was rich in carboxyl group and selected as carbon source, glycine (or PEG200, or hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was used as modification reagents to prepare the CDs with excellent properties by one-step microwave-assisted method. The prior CDs were selected and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), power X-ray diffraction (XRD), nuclear magnetic resonance (13CNMR) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) respectively. The cytotoxicity was also examined in Caco-2 cells. To investigate the potential application of CDs in vivo, the mode of interaction and mechanisms of CDs with human transferrin (hTf) was also discussed.

Citric acid was obtained from Tianjin Beifangtianyi Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Glycine and CTAB were purchased from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co. Ltd. PEG200 was obtained from Tianjin Guangpu Reagent Company. Human transferrin (A-1653, 96–99%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd (USA). All of the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi UV-3010 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). FT-IR spectroscopy investigation was accomplished by SHIMADIU FTIR-8900 equipment (Japan). XRD was collected in the range 10–70° using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Cu Ka radiation, λ=1.54178Å) (Germany). TEM measurement was performed on JEM 100SX (Japan) at the accelerating voltage of 200kV. NMR characterization was performed in D2O for 13CNMR (500.13MHz) on a Bruker DMX-500 spectrometer (Germany). All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

CDs were prepared by a single-step microwave-assisted method with citric acid as carbon source and glycine (or PEG200 and CTAB) as modification. In brief, citric acid (0.4g) and glycine (0.4g) were dissolved in 10mL distilled water, stirring for 10min. Then the solution was heated in microwave oven for 5min, during which the solution change from being a colourless liquid to a brownish red clustered solid, indicating the formation of CDs. Next, when the reactor was automatically cooled to the room temperature, the product was dissolved in water and centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min to remove the precipitate. Finally, the supernatant was subjected to dialysis for 48h using a 1000Da dialysis membrane against distilled water to remove unbounded modifications (dialysate was replaced once every 12h). The product dried at 80°C and CDs were obtained. CDs were donated as G-CDs, P-CDs and C-CDs according to different modifications (glycine, PEG200 and CTAB).

The QY of CDs was measured by the reference method (reference to quinine sulphate, Y=0.55) according to the following equation [33]:
Y u
=
Y s
·
F u
F s
·
A s
A u
Where Y is the quantum yield, F is the integrated area of the emission spectrum, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and the subscripts u and s refer to CDs and the standard, respectively.
Cell viability of CDs was evaluated on Caco-2 cells using MTT assay. Different concentrations of CDs (0.1mg/mL, 0.2mg/mL, 0.3mg/mL, 0.4mg/mL and 0.5mg/mL) were incubated in Caco-2 cells for 24h.

All calculations reported for hTf were in terms of hTf with the molecular weight of 80 KD. A fresh stock solution of hTf (5×10−6 mol/L−1) was prepared by directly dissolving the proteins in phosphate buffer solution (pH=7.4) for the fluorescence and UV–vis experiment.

The measured sample was prepared by mixing a quantity of G-CDs with hTf, and then diluted by PBS. The concentration of hTf was fixed at 5.0μM, and the concentration of CDs was varied from 0 to 25μM. The sample solutions were kept at room temperature for more than 12h before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection modification reagent and cell cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid was selected as carbon source and glycine (or PEG200 and CTAB) as modification reagents to prepare three carbon dots, G-CDs, P-CDs and C-CDs. Due to the different chemical structure of modification reagents; the three CDs had different spectral features and QYs (Table 1 and Fig. 1 (a)). Though the three CDs were all blue colours, they had different excitation and emission wavelengths; and G-CDs had the highest QY to 31%, which was higher than the previous reports [21]. Our results showed that modifications could influence the QY of CDs. Zhu reported that the introduction of amino group (or secondary amines) had a significant effect on the QY [2], our research confirms this viewpoint.

The cell cytotoxicity of the CDs was evaluated on Caco-2 cells using MTT assay (Fig. 1(b)). It showed that with the increasing of CDs’ concentration, G-CDs can promote the growth of the cells, P-CDs don’t affect the growth of cells obviously and cell survival rate was above 90% when CDs’ concentration was 0.4mg/mL; however, C-CDs was obviously toxic and seriously inhibits the growth of cells. It indicated that modification reagent could also influence the toxity of CDs significantly.

The microstructures of CDs were observed by TEM (Fig. 1(c)–(e)). Comparing to P-CDs and C-CDs, TEM micrograph of G-CDs was spherical shape and well dispersed, the average diameters of G-CDs were about 10nm, which was smaller than that of P-CDs and C-CDs.

The florescent stability of CDs at physiological condition was also detected (at different pH and NaCl concentrations). The results showed that the three CDs all could be used in physiological pH and ions strength (see Supporting information). Considering the QY, microstructure and cytotoxicity, we selected G-CDs with higher QY, smaller diameter, well dispersed and lower cytotoxity to characterization and carry out the next experiments.

G-CDs had a strong absorbance at 330nm in UV–vis spectrum and photoluminescence (PL) emission peak appeared at 409nm when excited at 335nm (Fig. 2 (a)). PL emission spectra were collected at various excitation wavelengths from 335 to 400nm; Fig. 2(b) showed that the maximum emission of PL spectra moved to longer wavelength with the increasing of excitation wavelength, the strongest peak shifts from 409nm to 475nm and the intensity decrease gradually as the excitation alters from 335nm to 400nm, which may be ascribed to the chemical nature and abundant surface defects of G-CDs [23].

FT-IR spectrum showed the structural insights about G-CDs (Fig. 2(c)): the broad peak at 3100–3500cm−1was due to stretching vibration of OH and NH, implying the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups and amino groups, the peak at 1645cm−1 attributed to the stretching of CO and 1100cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching. This information indicated that COOH, OH, and NH2 were present at the surface of G-CDs and these groups made G-CDs convenient to couple with other groups to be used in the future.

13CNMR spectroscopy was carried out to distinguish sp3-hybridized carbon atoms from sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the peak at 30–45ppm is corresponding to aliphatic sp3 carbon atoms; signals in the range of 90–185ppm indicate sp2 carbon atoms and 175–190ppm indicted carbonyl groups [34]. The XRD pattern of G-CDs (Fig. 2(e)) showed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ=20°–22° attributed to amorphous carbon.

To clearly observe the emitting light of G-CDs under fluorescence microscopy, G-CDs solution was dropped on lens paper (Fig. 3 ). We could see lens paper gave bright blue fluorescence under UV light, compared to the control, dropped with water.

Further study about the interaction and mechanisms of G-CDs with hTf was also discussed. The absorption intensity of hTf at 280nm increased and the peak position appeared blue shift slightly with the increasing concentration of G-CDs (Fig. 4 (a)), which maybe indicate that interaction of G-CDs with the ground state of hTf occurred. And then fluorescence spectroscopy was detected at the excitation wavelength 280nm where Trp and Tyr residues as endogenous fluorophore had fluorescence emission to further analysis the interaction mechanism of G-CDs with hTf.

A fixed concentration of hTf was titrated with different concentrations of G-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of hTf decreased along with a weaker blue shift of the maximum emission. This indicated that G-CDs bound to hTf by altering the micro environment around chromosphere residues; under the same conditions, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs gradually increased at the range of 360–550nm under the existence of protein (Fig. 4(b)). An isoemissive point appeared at 366nm, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of hTf depended on the formation of G-CDs-hTf complex. Moreover, the groups of −COOH, −OH, and −NH2 were present on the surface of G-CDs (Fig. 2(c)) and easily bound with hTf, which could occur energy transfer, result in making up defect and increase fluorescence intensity of G-CD. It’s possible that static quenching process was involved in G-CDs-hTf system.

To elucidate the type of fluorescence quenching mode based on the binding constant, the quenching of fluorescence of Tf with G-CDs was analysed according to the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1):
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]

F0 and F is fluorescence intensity when the quencher absent and present of the in the system respectively, Kq is the bimolecular quenching constant, τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of biological macromolecules when the quencher does not exist (τ 0 =10−8 s) [35], Kq
=4.56×1012 M−1 s−1 by linear regression of a plot of F0/F versus [Q] (the concentration of G-CDs) (Fig. 4(c)). It is far more less than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant, Kq of various quenchers with the biopolymer is 2×1010 M−1 s−1.
It suggests that fluorescence quenching is not dynamic quenching caused by the collision of molecules between G-CDs and hTf, but static quenching process by forming fluorescent complexes.

The fluorescence intensity can also be used to analyse the association constants (Ka ) and the number of binding sites (n) that can be obtained from the regression curve based on the following relation:
(2)
lg
F
0
− F
F
= lg
K a
+ n lg [ Q ]

On the other hand, the binding constant and binding sites give an expression to the binding rate in fact. The apparent binding rate is derived from a simple extension process based on G-CDs binding to hTf, which is a constant affinity process.

From Eq. (2), the values of Ka and n of G-CDs-hTf system were obtained to be 4.5×105 mol/L and 1.24 which can be rounded off to 1, respectively (Fig. 4(d)). These results may infer that there is a strong interaction between G-CDs and hTf, and one type of binding site for G-CDs in hTf when achieved conformation equilibrium. Then the binding between G-CDs and hTf could be considered as the result of apparent binding rate with high affinity process. The binding constant of the G-CDs shows higher binding affinity than that published values of small molecules that strongly bind to the same site of hTf, such as artemisinin (1.75×105mol/L) [36], querceti (2.09×105 mol/L) [37]. This shows that G-CDs have obvious advantages in interaction with hTf, compared to other small molecules, further implying the potential bio-compatible of G-CDs.

In summary, using different modification reagents, we synthesized three water-soluble fluorescent CDs (G-CDs, P-CDs C-CDs) by the single-step microwave-assisted method. Our results showed modification reagent can influence the structure, QY and cytotoxity of CDs, we choose G-CDs with higher QY smaller diameter, well dispersed and lower cytotoxity to do further experiments. G-CDs were about 10nm, amorphous, high QY (31%). There are two kinds of hybridized carbon, sp2 and sp3, in G-CDs. Moreover, the interaction mode and mechanism of G-CDs with hTf were studied. The data showed that the interaction of G-CDs with hTf was a static quenching process; there is a strong interaction between G-CDs and hTf, and one type of binding site for G-CDs in hTf. These results are contribute to prepare low cytotoxity, good biocompatible and high QY carbon dots, and establish the base of the potential application of CDs in vivo.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31201305).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2017.07.045.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
